description: Markets proposed in the academic literature that do not (yet) exist as liquid venues. Status pipeline: unbuilt -> attempted -> embryonic -> built_late. 'built_late' items are kept as calibration: they prove this list is a forward indicator, not a graveyard. Each item names the wall keeping it vacant; per the matrix-cell logic, contingent walls (liquidity bootstrap, regulation, computation) are opportunities, essential walls (one-sided flow, adverse selection) are warnings.

items.name: GDP-Linked Securities & Macro Markets
items.blurb: Standing markets in claims on national income — countries issue equity in themselves; investors and firms hedge recession risk directly.

literature.author: Robert Shiller
literature.work: Macro Markets: Creating Institutions for Managing Society's Largest Economic Risks

literature.author: Mark Kamstra & Robert Shiller
literature.work: The Case for Trills: Pricing the Government's New Debt Instrument

items.nearest_attempts: GDP warrants issued in sovereign restructurings (Argentina 2005, Greece 2012, Ukraine 2015) — one-off, illiquid, litigated.
items.wall: Requires sovereign issuance (governments resist selling upside); index revision risk; thin natural long side outside crises.
items.unlock: A G7 treasury issuing trills as standard debt management, or perp DEXs listing synthetic GDP perps (Hyperliquid's RWA perps are the closest live rail).

items.name: Lifetime Income / Occupational Hedging Markets
items.blurb: Markets in occupational income indices, letting a software engineer or radiologist hedge the secular decline of their profession.

literature.author: Robert Shiller
literature.work: The New Financial Order: Risk in the 21st Century

items.nearest_attempts: Nothing tradeable. AI-displacement anxiety has revived the idea in essays but no venue lists occupational income contracts.
items.wall: Moral hazard, index construction (whose income?), decades-long tenor, and everyone wants the same side of the trade.
items.unlock: Credible high-frequency occupational income indices (payroll-data vendors now have the raw material) plus an insurer or event-contract venue willing to warehouse the other side.

items.name: Household House-Price Hedging
items.blurb: Retail-accessible instruments to short your own city's housing index — insurance for the largest undiversified position most households hold.

literature.author: Karl Case, Robert Shiller & Allan Weiss
literature.work: Index-Based Futures and Options Markets in Real Estate

items.nearest_attempts: CME Case-Shiller housing futures (2006) — listed into the housing bubble's peak and languished at near-zero volume; home-equity-sharing fintechs hedge the level, not the index.
items.wall: Structurally one-sided: every homeowner is long and wants protection, no natural short of size; heavy basis risk between a metro index and your block.
items.unlock: Event-contract framing (monthly 'will the index print above X' markets) may bootstrap where futures failed — Kalshi already lists some housing markets; liquidity is the open question.

items.name: Futarchy / Decision Markets
items.blurb: Governance by conditional markets: trade 'value-if-policy-A' vs 'value-if-policy-B' and enact whichever the market prices higher.

literature.author: Robin Hanson
literature.work: Shall We Vote on Values, But Bet on Beliefs?

literature.author: Bo Cowgill & Eric Zitzewitz
literature.work: Corporate Prediction Markets: Evidence from Google, Ford, and Firm X
literature.note: Internal corporate markets were accurate — and were abandoned anyway. The wall is political, not informational.

items.nearest_attempts: MetaDAO (Solana) runs real futarchy for DAO treasury decisions; corporate internal markets (HP, Google, Ford) worked and were shut down.
items.wall: Conditional markets are thin; conditioning is not causation; and decision-makers dislike institutions that overrule them — a legitimacy wall, partly essential.
items.unlock: DAO governance is the natural sandbox: decisions are frequent, on-chain, and enforceable by code. Watch whether MetaDAO-style mechanisms survive their first adversarial epoch.

items.name: Frequent Batch Auction Venue
items.blurb: A major exchange that replaces continuous trading with uniform-price auctions every ~100ms, ending the latency arms race by design.

literature.author: Eric Budish, Peter Cramton & John Shim
literature.work: The High-Frequency Trading Arms Race: Frequent Batch Auctions as a Market Design Response

items.nearest_attempts: IEX's speed bump (a cousin, not the design); EBS latency floors; closing auctions prove batch mechanics work at scale once a day.
items.wall: Incumbent venue economics: exchanges monetize the arms race (colocation, proprietary feeds), so the parties who could adopt it profit from its absence — plus liquidity chicken-and-egg for any entrant.
items.unlock: A crypto or event-contract venue with no legacy colo revenue adopting FBA natively; on-chain block times already quantize trading, making DEXs the most plausible first movers.

items.name: Combinatorial Prediction Markets
items.blurb: Trade any logical combination of events ('Fed cuts AND recession by Q2') against one unified order book over the full joint distribution.

literature.author: Yiling Chen, Lance Fortnow, Nicolas Lambert, David Pennock & Jennifer Wortman
literature.note: Pricing general combinatorial markets is #P-hard — the wall, stated precisely.

items.nearest_attempts: Sportsbook same-game parlays are priced combinatorially but house-banked, not exchange-traded; current event venues list each market independently, leaving cross-market coherence to arbitrageurs.
items.wall: Computation (#P-hard in general) and UX; tractable restricted structures exist in the literature but no venue has shipped them.
items.unlock: Restricted combinatorial structures (trees, small Bayes nets) are tractable per the same literature; an AI-agent-heavy venue is the natural adopter since bots, not humans, would quote the book.

items.name: Liquid Longevity Risk Market
items.blurb: Standardized, exchange-traded mortality/longevity instruments letting pensions lay off the risk that retirees live longer than expected.

literature.author: David Blake & William Burrows
literature.work: Survivor Bonds: Helping to Hedge Mortality Risk

items.nearest_attempts: EIB/BNP longevity bond (2004) withdrawn for lack of demand; OTC longevity swaps and q-forwards trade between pensions and reinsurers — bespoke, illiquid, dealer-bound.
items.wall: One-sided flow again: pensions, annuity writers, and individuals all want the same protection; only reinsurers naturally take the other side, and they prefer bespoke deals.
items.unlock: Capital-markets investors treating longevity as an uncorrelated carry asset class (the cat-bond playbook, which took 15 years to liquefy hurricane risk).

items.name: Human Capital Equity / Income-Share Markets
items.blurb: Equity-like financing of education and careers — investors fund training for a fraction of future income, with a secondary market in the claims.

literature.author: Milton Friedman
literature.work: The Role of Government in Education
literature.note: Proposed equity finance for human capital and named the wall in the same essay: 'the analogue of indentured servitude' optics plus adverse selection.

items.nearest_attempts: Income-share agreements: Purdue's Back-a-Boiler (paused), coding-bootcamp ISAs (collapsed amid regulatory action); no secondary market ever formed.
items.wall: Adverse selection (high earners refuse the deal), servicing costs, and consumer-protection regulation treating ISAs as disguised loans.
items.unlock: Probably arrives, if ever, as pooled/securitized exposure rather than per-person claims — the same de-personalization that made mortgages tradeable.

items.name: Geopolitical / Policy Risk Markets (DARPA PAM)
items.blurb: Markets on geopolitical events and policy outcomes for forecasting and hedging — the 'terrorism futures' DARPA cancelled in 48 hours of headlines.
items.nearest_attempts: Built, ~22 years after cancellation: Kalshi and Polymarket now run deep markets on strikes, ceasefires, elections, and policy decisions, cited by newsrooms as probability sources.
items.wall: Was purely political legitimacy — which is why it fell the moment venues with their own licenses (rather than a government sponsor) listed the contracts.
items.unlock: Done. Kept here as calibration: the list you are reading is a forward indicator with multi-decade lead times, not a graveyard.

items.name: True Exchanges for Data
items.blurb: A real two-sided market where datasets price-discover like commodities, rather than catalog-style stores with posted prices.

literature.author: Kenneth Arrow
literature.work: Economic Welfare and the Allocation of Resources for Invention
literature.note: The information paradox: a buyer can't value information without receiving it, and having received it, needn't pay — the essential wall, stated 60 years ago.

items.nearest_attempts: Ocean Protocol and a decade of 'data marketplace' startups; Nasdaq Data Link is a successful store, which is exactly the point — stores work, exchanges don't.
items.wall: Likely essential, not contingent: non-rival goods with Arrow's paradox and severe adverse selection resist double-auction price formation. Mirrors the structural matrix cell at Alt Data x Venue.
items.unlock: Possibly none. Included as the control case — the literature also tells you which empty cells to leave alone.
